---
title: "HW6"
author: "Isabella Fratantonio"
output: html_notebook
---
```{r}
library(ggplot2)
```

## Problem 2
# part a

```{r}
set.seed(42)

# parameters
mu <- 1
sigma <- 2
n <- 10000
```

```{r}
p <- function(theta) {
  (1 / (sqrt(2 * pi) * sigma)) * exp(-(log(theta / (1 - theta)) - mu)^2 / (2 * sigma^2)) * (1 / (theta * (1 - theta)))
}

# define the proposal distribution 
q <- function(theta) {
  dbeta(theta, 1, 1)
}

k <- 5
```

```{r}
# verification
theta_vals <- seq(0.001, 0.999, length.out = 1000)
p_vals <- p(theta_vals)
kq_vals <- k * q(theta_vals)

plot(theta_vals, p_vals, type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = "Density",
     main = "Verification: kq(v) >= p(v)",
     ylim = c(0, max(c(p_vals, kq_vals))))
lines(theta_vals, kq_vals, col = "red", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("p(theta)", "k * q(theta)"),
       col = c("blue", "red"), lty = 1, lwd = 2)
```

```{r}
# rejection sampling 
rejection_sampling_logit_normal <- function(n) {
  samples <- c()
  while (length(samples) < n) {
    v <- runif(1) 
    if (k * q(v) * runif(1) < p(v)) {
      samples <- c(samples, v)
    }
  }
  return(samples)
}

theta_samples <- rejection_sampling_logit_normal(n)
```

```{r}
# verify 
ggplot(data.frame(theta = theta_samples), aes(x = theta)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50, fill = "blue", alpha = 0.5) +
  stat_function(fun = p, col = "red", lwd = 1) +
  labs(title = "Rejection Sampling from Logit-Normal Prior",
       x = expression(theta), y = "Density")
```

# part b
```{r}
y_samples <- rbinom(n, size = 1, prob = theta_samples)

cat("Number of y=0:", sum(y_samples == 0), "\n")
cat("Number of y=1:", sum(y_samples == 1), "\n")
```

```{r}
# visualization 1: theoretical joint distribution 
theta_grid <- seq(0.001, 0.999, length.out = 200)


joint_y0 <- (1 - theta_grid) * p(theta_grid)
joint_y1 <- theta_grid * p(theta_grid)

plot(theta_grid, joint_y0, type = "l", col = "blue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = expression(theta), ylab = expression(p(y, theta)),
     main = "Theoretical Joint Distribution",
     ylim = c(0, max(c(joint_y0, joint_y1))))
lines(theta_grid, joint_y1, col = "red", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("y = 0", "y = 1"),
       col = c("blue", "red"), lty = 1, lwd = 2)
```
```{r}
# visualization 2: empirical joint distribution from ancestral samples
joint_df <- data.frame(theta = theta_samples, y = as.factor(y_samples))

ggplot(joint_df, aes(x = theta, fill = y)) +
  geom_histogram(position = "stack", bins = 50, alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("blue", "red")) +
  labs(title = "Empirical Joint Distribution from Ancestral Sampling",
       x = expression(theta), y = "Frequency")
```
```{r}
# The sampler did a good job because the empirical histogram matches the
# theoretical joint. y=1 samples concentrate at higher theta and y=0 samples
# concentrate at lower theta which expected from the bernoulli.
```
```{r}
# The next steps are to find the posterior and filter the joint samples
# to keep only those where y matches the observed value. Since
# p(theta|y) is proportional to p(y, theta), the distribution of the filtered
# theta values approximates the posterior. If we observe y = 0, the relevant 
# samples approximate p(theta|y = 0).
```
```{r}
# estimated posteriors
theta_given_y0 <- theta_samples[y_samples == 0]
theta_given_y1 <- theta_samples[y_samples == 1]

ggplot(data.frame(theta = theta_given_y0), aes(x = theta)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50, fill = "blue", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Estimated Posterior p(theta | y = 0)",
       x = expression(theta), y = "Density")
```
```{r}
ggplot(data.frame(theta = theta_given_y1), aes(x = theta)) +
  geom_histogram(aes(y = after_stat(density)), bins = 50, fill = "red", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Estimated Posterior p(theta | y = 1)",
       x = expression(theta), y = "Density")
```
```{r}
cat("Estimated posterior mean for theta|y=0:", mean(theta_given_y0), "\n")
cat("Estimated posterior mean for theta|y=1:", mean(theta_given_y1), "\n")
```

## Problem 3
# part a

```{r}
library(mvtnorm)

# target distribution
p <- function(y, mu, Sigma) {
  dmvnorm(y, mean = mu, sigma = Sigma)
}

# metropolis sampler for bivariate normal
gaussian_metropolis <- function(n, mu, Sigma, sigma = 2, burn = 0.18) {
  samples <- list()
  y1 <- 0.0
  y2 <- 0.0
  total_samples <- floor(n / (1 - burn))
  m <- floor(total_samples * burn)
  accepted <- 0

  while (length(samples) < total_samples) {
    y1_prop <- rnorm(1, y1, sigma)
    y2_prop <- rnorm(1, y2, sigma)
    alpha <- p(c(y1_prop, y2_prop), mu, Sigma) / p(c(y1, y2), mu, Sigma)

    if (runif(1) < alpha) {
      y1 <- y1_prop
      y2 <- y2_prop
      accepted <- accepted + 1
    }
    samples[[length(samples) + 1]] <- c(y1, y2)
  }

  acceptance_rate <- accepted / total_samples
  cat(sprintf("Acceptance rate: %.3f\n", acceptance_rate))

  samples <- do.call(rbind, samples)
  samples <- samples[(m + 1):nrow(samples), ]
  return(samples)
}
```

# part b
```{r}
# target parameters
mu <- c(-4, 6)
Sigma <- matrix(c(1, -0.6, -0.6, 1), nrow = 2, ncol = 2)

samples_3 <- gaussian_metropolis(n = 10000, mu = mu, Sigma = Sigma, sigma = 1.25)
```

```{r}
# verify convergence
plot(samples_3[, 1], type = "l", main = "Convergence of Y1 Samples",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "Y1")
```

```{r}
plot(samples_3[, 2], type = "l", main = "Convergence of Y2 Samples",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "Y2")
```

```{r}
# check ACF plots
acf(samples_3[, 1], main = "Metropolis Y1 Samples ACF Plot")
```

```{r}
acf(samples_3[, 2], main = "Metropolis Y2 Samples ACF Plot")
```

```{r}
{
# estimated mean and covariance
cat("Estimated mean:\n")
print(colMeans(samples_3))
cat("\nTrue mean:\n")
print(mu)

cat("\nEstimated covariance:\n")
print(cov(samples_3))
cat("\nTrue covariance:\n")
print(Sigma)

}
```

```{r}
# The estimated mean and covariance are very close to the true parameters.
# The convergence plots show the chain stabilized immediately and the
# acceptance rate of 40% is in a good range. The ACF plots show
# some autocorrelation but it decreases around lag 25. You can trust these 
# samples came from the target distribution.
```

## Problem 4
# part a
```{r}
# observed data
y <- c(-2, 1)
rho <- -0.6
n <- 10000

# gibbs sampler for the joint posterior
gibbs_posterior <- function(n, y, rho, burn = 0.18) {
  total_samples <- floor(n / (1 - burn))
  m <- floor(total_samples * burn)
  samples <- matrix(0, nrow = total_samples, ncol = 2)
  
  # initialize
  mu1 <- 0
  mu2 <- 0
  
  for (i in 1:total_samples) {
    mu1 <- rnorm(1, mean = y[1] + rho * (mu2 - y[2]), sd = sqrt(1 - rho^2))
    mu2 <- rnorm(1, mean = y[2] + rho * (mu1 - y[1]), sd = sqrt(1 - rho^2))
    samples[i, ] <- c(mu1, mu2)
  }
  samples <- samples[(m + 1):total_samples, ]
  return(samples)
}

samples_4 <- gibbs_posterior(n, y, rho)
```

```{r}
# verify convergence
plot(samples_4[, 1], type = "l", main = "Convergence of mu1 Samples",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "mu1")
```

```{r}
plot(samples_4[, 2], type = "l", main = "Convergence of mu2 Samples",
     xlab = "Iteration", ylab = "mu2")
```

```{r}
# check ACF plots
acf(samples_4[, 1], main = "Gibbs mu1 Samples ACF Plot")
```
```{r}
acf(samples_4[, 2], main = "Gibbs mu2 Samples ACF Plot")
```
```{r}
# the acf plots show the autocorrelation drops to zero almost immediately which means the gibbs sampler # is mixing very well. much better than metropolis since gibbs samples directly from the conditional 
# posteriors.
``` 

# part b
```{r}
{
cat("estimated mean:\n")
print(colMeans(samples_4))
cat("\ntrue posterior mean (= y):\n")
print(y)

cat("\nestimated covariance:\n")
print(cov(samples_4))
cat("\ntrue posterior covariance:\n")
print(matrix(c(1, rho, rho, 1), nrow = 2))
}
```

```{r}
# The estimated mean and covariance match the true posterior parameters. 
# An improper uniform prior has the benefit of being noninformative so the
# posterior is based on only the data. The drawback is that it can lead
# to improper posteriors in some models but with a normal likelihood
# the posterior is still proper.
```
